exemplar.title: M25 #6: Does acquiring knowledge destroy our sense of wonder? Discuss with reference to two areas of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: M25 #6: Does acquiring knowledge destroy our sense of wonder? Discuss with reference to two areas of knowledge.

strengths: Sustained and focused engagement with the prescribed title, with clearly articulated links to the human sciences and mathematics (Criterion A).

Coherent and well-supported argumentation, using a rich variety of specific examples (Arab Spring anecdote; Kahneman & Tversky; Keplerâs snowflake; PoincarÃ© Conjecture) and effective counter-claims (Criterion B).
Logical overall structure with a clear introduction and conclusion that frame the discussion and synthesize implications (Criterion C).
Professional academic tone and formal presentation, including a strong conceptual re-definition of âwonderâ that grounds the essay (Criterion D).
weaknesses: Occasional lapses in structural coherence (abrupt shift between areas of knowledge; under-developed transitions) limit the flow (Criterion C).
Minor precision issues in language (redundancies, vague phrasing) and typographical errors detract slightly from clarity (Criterion D).
Lack of explicit framing of a central knowledge question in TOK terminology weakens analytical focus (Criterion A).
Some evaluative depths remain under-explored (e.g., implications for educational policy; deeper critique of broad generalizations in the human sciences) (Criterion B).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: In order to reverse-engineer the official total mark of 8, we have apportioned marks across the four TOK criteria in proportion to the essayâs demonstrated strengths and weaknesses. Criterion A (Understanding Knowledge Questions): 2/2, reflecting sustained focus but missing an explicit TOK-framed knowledge question. Criterion B (Quality of Analysis and Use of Examples): 3/3, given the coherent, nuanced arguments supported by highly relevant, discipline-specific examples, despite some areas needing deeper evaluation. Criterion C (Organization): 2/2, for a clear overall structure with only minor lapses in transitions. Criterion D (Style and Presentation): 1/1, acknowledging the professional tone and presentation but noting language imprecision and citation oversights. These allocations sum exactly to the total mark of 8, in line with the examinerâs final judgment and the strand-level assessments provided.

annotations.comment: The essay would benefit from explicitly linking Keplerâs findings back to the central knowledge question about wonder to reinforce analytical cohesion.
The phrase âstep from initial axioms to final conclusionsâ is vague; specifying key logical steps in the proof would clarify the discussion of methodological certainty.
The clear formatting of the title page establishes a professional presentation, reinforcing the formal academic tone of the essay.
The essay immediately presents the prescribed title prominently, demonstrating a sustained and focused engagement with the TOK prompt.
By specifying the two areas of knowledgeâHuman sciences and mathematicsâthe student sets a clear scope for analysis, ensuring relevance to the title.
The student offers a nuanced redefinition of âsense of wonderâ that expands beyond mere curiosity, laying a strong conceptual foundation for the discussion.
The phrase âby bringing light to light the complexityâ is redundant and unclear; consider revising to improve precision and readability.
The thesis statement is lengthy and dense; condensing it into a more concise claim would enhance clarity and reader engagement.
The shift from human sciences to mathematics is abrupt; adding a clearer transitional sentence would improve the essayâs structural coherence.
The conclusion reiterates earlier points but adds limited new insight; consider including specific implications for educational practice or policy.
Explicitly framing the central knowledge question in TOK terminology here would anchor the subsequent analysis more effectively.
The essay does not explicitly articulate a central knowledge question in TOK terminology; framing one would strengthen the analytical focus.
This sentence ends abruptly, suggesting a missing clause; ensure each paragraph completes its line of reasoning to maintain coherence.
The Arab Spring anecdote is a vivid, discipline-specific example that effectively links generalisation in human sciences to the diminished sense of wonder.
Laplaceâs quote is integrated smoothly to introduce a counter-argument, demonstrating depth in the discussion of complexity in the human sciences.
Contrasting novice enthusiasm with expert apathy deepens the evaluation of how accumulating knowledge can induce predictability and reduce awe.
Discussing the methodological implications of relying on a single classroom anecdote would enhance the rigor of the human sciences investigation.
The Laplace quotation lacks a specific citation in the text; ensure all direct quotes are properly referenced to uphold academic integrity.
The analysis remains largely descriptive; engaging more critically with the validity of broad generalisations across different social contexts would strengthen the argument.
The conclusionâs synthesis of methodology, motivation and implication provides a coherent evaluative perspective on the relationship between knowledge and wonder.
The final paragraph effectively closes the discussion with an epistemological reflection that links wonder to ongoing curiosity and engagement.
The examination of empirical methods and cognitive-bias research shows strong methodological awareness and supports the counter-claim effectively.
The Kepler snowflake example is an exemplary choice that vividly illustrates intrinsic mathematical curiosity driving discovery.
The critique of mathematical certainty could be deepened by discussing alternative proof approaches (e.g., computational or exploratory) that preserve a sense of wonder.
Linking Keplerâs work to later mathematicians such as Descartes effectively demonstrates the continuity and generative nature of mathematical inquiry.
The PoincarÃ© Conjecture is an excellent example illustrating how resolving a deep mathematical mystery can advance knowledge yet diminish the original sense of wonder.
Typographical errors such as âcreatorâ instead of âcreateâ appear; a careful proofread would improve clarity and precision.
Reflecting on why intrigue can persist after a proofâs resolution (for example, through its impact on subsequent research) would add valuable evaluative depth.
The assertion that wonder is transformed rather than destroyed encapsulates the essayâs nuanced argument, demonstrating a strong evaluative stance.

The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are

examples.

different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
